title: Chief Marketing Officer
dossier_summary: Jon Burke was promoted from VP of Marketing to CMO in late September 2025, days before Qdoba debuted its first-ever national campaign on Oct 1, 2025 (set to Hall and Oates' 'You Make My Dreams,' spotlighting free guac and queso, running through 2026 across streaming, social, online video and live football). Qdoba is positioning as the anti-Chipotle (number two in a category Burke himself calls thinly competitive), targeting 100 annual openings by 2027 and a doubled footprint by 2032 with 650+ signed development commitments. In a Marketing Dive interview, Burke noted prior ownership wanted to hide the free guac/queso offer 'versus celebrating it.'
email1_body: jon, the line you gave Marketing Dive about prior ownership wanting to hide the free guac and queso 'versus celebrating it' is the most honest thing a fast-casual CMO has said out loud this year. it also explains why the Hall and Oates spot lands the way it does: you finally have a distinctive brand asset on TV instead of buried on a menu board. with the campaign running into 2026 and 650+ units in the pipeline, the harder question is whether share of voice keeps pace with the build. how are you thinking about that gap?
email2_body: one more thought on the doubling-by-2032 plan. the category entry points for fast-casual mexican still default to Chipotle by reflex, even in markets where you outnumber them. that is a brand memory problem, not a media problem. curious whether the football flights are getting tested against unaided recall lifts in your priority markets (Florida, Texas, Tennessee), or mostly traffic.
email3_subject: number two in a category of one
email3_body: your QSR Magazine framing of 'flame-grilled, fresh prep, free guac and queso' as the things that travel is the right shortlist. the risk for any number-two in a category Chipotle defines is that distinctive brand assets get read as variations of the leader rather than their own thing. Moe's lost that fight on queso for the same reason. how are you protecting flame-grilled from becoming a footnote?
email4_body: last note, jon. Mythic runs a Growth Codes audit using public data, brand tracking and media signals to surface the decisions quietly suppressing topline growth at brands scaling franchise footprint. Scott Luther walks it in 30 minutes. we have done versions for Subway, Meineke and Harley-Davidson. if not you on the Qdoba side, who should I send it to?

title: EVP & Head of Marketing
dossier_summary: Stephen Kennedy joined Noodles & Company as EVP Marketing in late 2024 after a Domino's run, walking into a brand in active turnaround. Q1 2026 posted +9% systemwide comps and tripled adjusted EBITDA, but the cost has been 42 closures in 2025 and another 30-35 planned for 2026, shrinking the footprint ~18% in two years.
email1_body: stephen, the line you used about noodle-centric messaging reinforcing the authority of the brand stuck with me. it is the right instinct in a category where panera owns soup-and-sandwich and chipotle owns the bowl. the hard part is rebuilding distinctive brand assets and category entry points while the footprint is still contracting 18 percent over two years. when share of voice is uneven against those two, what is the read inside on whether brand memory is recovering at the pace comps are?
email2_body: one more thought. the +9 percent Q1 comp is traffic-led, not price, which usually means the menu relaunch is doing work the media mix is not getting credit for. easy place for suppressed growth to hide. worth a look at whether paid is reaching the lapsed user or just the loyal one?
email3_body: the fast casual segment is projected to add 84 billion in US spend through 2029, almost all of it going to brands with clear category entry points. cava, sweetgreen, chipotle each own one. noodles arguably owns comfort, but it is under-claimed in the media. curious if that is on the roadmap for the back half.
email4_body: stephen, last note. we built a free Growth Codes audit at Mythic that uses public data to surface the few decisions quietly suppressing topline growth in brand and performance. scott luther would walk you through what we see at noodles in 30 minutes, no pitch attached. clients include subway, metlife, ally. worth a look?

title: Chief Marketing Officer
dossier_summary: Denise stepped into the Portillo's CMO seat in Sept 2025 from Marco's Pizza, walking into a Q1 2026 print with same-store sales down 0.1%, AUVs sliding from $8.7M to $8.3M as new-unit honeymoons taper, and a board that publicly admitted Portillo's has 80% brand awareness in Chicago but only 20% in Houston and 30% in Dallas.
email1_body: denise, the gap your board chair flagged on the last call -- 80% awareness in chicago, 20% in houston, 30% in dallas -- is the cleanest framing of the texas situation i've seen anyone at portillo's put out loud. most of the press is still treating it as a cluster and real estate story. the honeymoon taper from $8.7M to $8.3M AUVs reads more like a brand memory problem than a footprint one. curious whether you're seeing the same thing inside the building, or whether the read from the perks data is telling you something different about who actually knows portillo's outside chicagoland?
email2_body: one more thought. the eight-unit 2026 plan plus the smaller 5,500 sq ft prototype effectively buys you a year of breathing room on capex, but it also means topline growth has to come from existing restaurants and perks rather than new openings. perks closing 2025 at 2M members and ~10% of sales is a real distinctive brand asset to build off. is the 2026 plan to lean into that base, or to widen reach in the new markets first?
email3_subject: italian beef as a category entry point
email3_body: denise, watched raising cane's and culver's both spend years buying share of voice in markets before opening, not after. portillo's seems to be doing the reverse -- opening first, then trying to explain italian beef to a houston guest who has no category entry point for it. the dfw airport opening could actually flip that dynamic if it's used as a brand impression machine rather than a revenue unit. is that how the team is framing it internally?
email4_body: denise, won't keep bumping this. you walked into a hard quarter and a real brand-vs-footprint puzzle, and the people best positioned to crack it are the ones already inside. if it's not a fit, no worries at all. if it is, scott on my side has done this exercise for spectrum, metlife, and harley and would happily walk you through what the public data on portillo's actually says in 30 minutes. if not us, who are you talking to about the awareness side?

title: Chief Marketing Officer
dossier_summary: Potbelly is mid-breakout: 50 new shops planned for 2026 with 35 of them franchise, the 500th shop milestone this year, and a stated path to 2,000+ locations following the RaceTrac acquisition in Oct 2025. David Daniels (CMO since Aug 2021, ex-ABInBev / Pizza Hut) is driving a franchisee-first, hyper-localized marketing model on top of $1.3M AUVs and 3M+ Potbelly Perks members.
email1_body: david, going from 440 to 500 shops while signing 43 multi-unit developers is the kind of footprint move that usually outruns brand memory. most sandwich chains hit that inflection and find their share of voice still priced for the old system size, which quietly suppresses topline growth in every new trade area a franchisee opens. when you look at the 2026 plan against subway and jersey mike's media weight in the markets you're entering, where do you think potbelly's distinctive brand assets are doing the most work and where are they thinnest?
email2_body: one more thought, david. the franchisee-first hyper-local model you've been building since 2021 is the right instinct, but it tends to fragment category entry points right when you need them consolidated for the 2,000-shop story. curious whether the racetrac chapter is changing how you weight national brand spend against local franchisee co-op in the 2026 media mix.
email3_subject: what scott would actually look at
email3_body: david, scott luther on our side ran marketing at firehouse subs through a similar franchise scale curve, so when he looks at potbelly he's reading specific things: perks penetration by daypart, AUV variance between company and franchise shops, and where brand memory is suppressing trial in new DMAs. happy to have him walk you through a free growth codes audit on potbelly, 30 minutes, no deck theater. worth a look?
email4_subject: re: shop 500 and share of voice
email4_body: david, last note. if 2026 isn't the year to pressure-test brand work against the franchise expansion, totally fair. we do this kind of audit for spectrum, metlife, ally, subway, and meineke, so the QSR franchise pattern is well worn for us. if it makes sense later in the year as the new shops open, my line stays open.

title: Director of Media, Social, and Local Restaurant Marketing
dossier_summary: Kelsey is Sr. Manager of National Media at Qdoba, sitting inside the new national-media build-out under CMO Jon Burke. Qdoba is opening 52 units in 2025, 75 in 2026, 100+ in 2027 across wildly mixed DMAs (Salt Lake, Albuquerque, Hudson Valley, PA/SNJ, Tennessee, Carolinas) with 600+ restaurants in the development pipeline.
email1_body: kelsey, the doherty 27-unit build in hudson valley and the rose group 35 in pa/snj are going to land in dmas with totally different share-of-voice dynamics than denver or minneapolis where qdoba already has density. curious how you're thinking about market-level media weighting when a dma goes from 2 stores to 12 over 24 months. is the co-op budget scaling linearly with footprint, or are you front-loading attention-based planning in the new dmas before the openings hit?
email2_body: the piece i keep getting stuck on with multi-dma qsr media is paid search auction pressure. in your mature dmas chipotle and moe's are bidding against you on branded and category terms, but in the new pa/snj markets it's a different auction entirely. are you running separate sem bid logic by dma maturity tier, or is it one national structure with geo modifiers?
email3_body: the chip pants nyfw moment got the earned media spike but the harder problem is what comes 6 weeks after a stunt like that in a dma where you have 3 stores vs 30. mythic builds a free growth codes audit that maps geographic media concentration gaps against store density. scott luther ran qsr media before this and walks through it in 30 minutes. worth a look?
email4_subject: re: doherty + rose group dma question
email4_body: kelsey, last note from me on this. with 75 openings landing in 2026 across mixed-maturity dmas, the media mix question only gets harder. if the growth codes audit isn't useful timing-wise i get it. if you'd rather i send the dma concentration framework over as a one-pager instead of a call, happy to do that.
